Deeper Reflections

What Boredom
Taught Us

Before the phone in the pocket and the screen in the palm, there was the long afternoon — and the education it quietly delivered to everyone who survived it.

A reflection on patience, solitude, and the lost art of going somewhere inside your own head

Rows  ·  Summer  ·  Somewhere in the middle of a very long day

The summer I was a teenager, someone paid me to walk rows of bean fields with a machete and cut weeds. That was the job in its entirety. Long green rows stretching to the tree line. The sun making the air thick. The repetitive sound of the blade through the stems. And me, alone with all of it, for hours at a stretch, with no company but the heat and the work and the enormous, unbroken silence of my own mind.

I did not know it then — you never know it then — but I was learning something. Not something that could be graded or certified or put on a resume. Something older than that. Something the long days teach, if you let them: how to be alone without being lonely. How to be idle without being empty. How to take the raw material of boredom and build, inside it, whole worlds of your own construction.

Those fields were where I learned to daydream properly. Not the distracted, half-attentive drifting that happens when a meeting runs long, but the real thing — sustained, inhabited, almost architectural. You'd walk a row, and somewhere around the third or fourth hundred steps, the present moment would go quiet, and you'd slip out of it. Into a story. Into a conversation you'd been meaning to have. Into an invention, a plan, a memory turned over and examined like a stone in the hand. You were there in the field, but you were also, entirely, somewhere else.

The boredom wasn't the problem to be solved. It was the condition under which something else became possible. It was the silence before the music.

What We've Forgotten About Empty Time

There is a kind of boredom we don't much encounter anymore, and its absence has cost us something we haven't fully named yet. It was the boredom of waiting rooms without phones. Of long car rides with nothing but the passing fields and the radio your parents controlled. Of summer afternoons that had no scheduled activity, no camp, no structured enrichment — just the hours, loose and unspent, yours to do something with.

Previous generations lived inside that boredom the way fish live inside water. It was the medium of ordinary life. You waited for things. You sat. You let your mind do what it wanted. And what it wanted, it turns out, was to work — quietly, privately, on nothing in particular and everything at once.

Children who were bored became children who read for hours, not because reading was assigned but because a book was the most exciting exit available. They built things, poorly at first and then less poorly. They had long, strange, elaborate conversations with each other about subjects that mattered enormously in the moment and have been entirely forgotten since. They developed what might be called an inner life — a populated, furnished, surprisingly comfortable interior where they could live for stretches of time without needing anything from outside.

Some of what you built in those fields was trivial. Idle fantasy, mildly embarrassing in retrospect. But some of it wasn't. In the absence of distraction, the mind gravitates toward the things it actually cares about — the unfinished thought, the half-formed hope, the question that hasn't yet found its words. Boredom was the space where those things surfaced.

The machete rose and fell. The row went on. And somewhere in the middle of it, you'd find yourself working something out — something you didn't even know needed working out until the quiet gave it room.

The Mercy of Distraction, and Its Price

It would be dishonest to pretend that boredom was only ennobling. It wasn't. It was also tedious, itchy, slow, and occasionally maddening in the particular way that only enforced idleness can produce. There were days in the field when the row seemed to have no end, when the heat was personal and mean, when the mind couldn't find purchase on anything interesting and just turned in small, unhappy circles. Those were the days that taught patience — not as a virtue you cultivated, but as a thing that was simply required of you, the way gravity is required.

Patience used to be installed through boredom the way a skill is installed through repetition. You endured the long wait, and the long wait eventually ended, and you were slightly more capable of endurance than you had been before. This happened to nearly everyone. It happened without anyone meaning to teach it.

We have built a world in which it almost never happens anymore. The phone fills every gap. The gap was where the patience was being manufactured.

The Emptiness of Time
An hour with nothing in it feels different when you're twelve than it does when you're forty. At twelve, it feels vast — almost frightening in its open expanse. Learning to inhabit that vastness without panic is one of the quiet educations that empty time provides.
Productive Daydreaming
The daydream gets a bad reputation as idle escapism. But the mind at play is not the mind at rest — it's the mind working sideways, making connections, trying on possibilities, rehearsing futures. The bean field rows were as productive as any classroom, just in a language nobody was grading.
Solitude Without Loneliness
There's a difference, and it takes time alone to learn it. Loneliness is the ache of unwanted separateness. Solitude is the ease of chosen privacy. The long afternoon taught you, slowly, that your own company was sufficient — that the mind was, on its own, a reasonably interesting place to be.
The Permission of Uselessness
Nothing produced in a daydream is useful in any obvious sense. Nobody needed the story you built between rows fourteen and twenty-two. And yet something was built — something interior, something structural. The willingness to let the mind wander without demanding it produce anything is a form of trust we're losing practice with.

What the Screen Has Changed

The phone did not create impatience. But it removed the conditions under which patience was quietly required. It is hard to blame it — distraction, offered freely and immediately, is very difficult to refuse. The waiting room fills with bent necks and bright screens. The long drive disappears into podcasts and playlists. The gaps are closed, one by one, by a device engineered specifically to close them.

What goes with the gaps is harder to describe than to feel. It's the low-level comfort with stillness that accumulated, unnoticed, across all those enforced idle hours of childhood and adolescence. The capacity to be alone with your thoughts without immediately needing to do something about them. The ability to follow a daydream to its end rather than abandoning it at the first flicker of boredom, because there is nothing to abandon it for.

Young people today are not lazier or less curious than previous generations. They are, in many measurable ways, more informed and more connected. But they have grown up without the long, structureless hours that used to be the furnace where a certain kind of interior life was forged. Whether that matters — and how much — is a question we are only beginning to understand, and will probably not fully understand until the generation growing up now is old enough to look back.

We gave children everything to do. We may have inadvertently taken from them the experience of having nothing to do — and all the quiet competence that comes from learning to survive it.

The Field That Was Also a Classroom

I did not go back to those fields. The job was one summer, and then it was over, and there were other summers with other jobs and other forms of tedium, each of which taught something I didn't know I was learning. But I think about the rows sometimes — their long, straight patience, the way each one looked exactly like the last and yet had to be walked through independently, without shortcuts.

What I learned there wasn't in any curriculum. Nobody assigned it. Nobody assessed it. There was no rubric for the quality of your daydreams, no grade for endurance, no certificate for having discovered that your own mind was livable. You just walked the row. You did it again. You let the hours be what they were — empty and full at the same time, the way a field looks bare in early spring and is, underground, furiously alive.

Boredom, the old kind, the unmedicated kind, the kind that had no remedy except to wait it out — it was a patient teacher. It didn't explain itself. It just sat there, long and hot and necessary, and waited to see what you'd make of it.

Most of us made something. We just didn't know it at the time.

The rows had no end you could see from where you stood. You took the next step anyway. That, too, was the lesson.
